data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Health dashboards work better with high contrast and card-based layouts to make data scannable and less overwhelming
facade.label: Dark cards, light background
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers clear visual separation; probably likes cards, containers, or other bounded data blocks
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer softer contrast, unified fields, or a more minimalist single-color approach to information display

evidence.hypothesis: Health dashboards work better with high contrast and card-based layouts to make data scannable and less overwhelming
evidence.implication: User prefers clear visual separation; probably likes cards, containers, or other bounded data blocks

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: The user might prefer a sparse, numbers-first layout that reduces visual noise and emphasizes raw metrics over visual flourish—testing whether they lean toward digital clarity.
facade.label: Minimal data view
facade.acceptImplies: User favors clean, data-dense presentation; likely prefers tables, numbers, and restrained color over illustrations or skeuomorphic warmth.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants more visual breathing room, context, or humanizing elements (charts, icons, encouraging language) to make data feel less cold or clinical.

facade.hypothesis: A friendly, personality-driven tone with casual language and warmth will feel more encouraging and motivating for long-term health tracking than a neutral, clinical approach.
facade.label: Warm and conversational
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers a supportive, approachable voice—use conversational copy, encouragement, maybe light humor. The dashboard should feel like a health buddy, not a medical record.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clean professionalism and clarity over personality. Lean into clarity, precision, and neutral tone. Let the data speak without emotional scaffolding.

facade.hypothesis: Users want prominent metrics (like 'Days streak: 47') front-and-center, even without trend lines or comparisons that explain *why* the number matters.
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data vs key metrics — specifically, metric prominence vs interpretive scaffolding
facade.label: Big numbers, small context
facade.acceptImplies: Show primary stats in large, isolated cards. Users will fill in meaning themselves or accept ambiguous metrics as motivational.
facade.rejectImplies: Users need mini-narratives around numbers: sparklines, change badges, goal progress bars, or text like '↑8% from last week' to make metrics *legible*. Raw numbers alone feel hollow or anxiety-inducing.

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid, continuous motion over instant jumps when reviewing experiment history and metrics
facade.axisTargeted: Animated continuity vs instant state changes
facade.label: Smooth scrolling
facade.acceptImplies: Design uses eased transitions, momentum scrolling, and gradual reveals for data updates throughout the dashboard
facade.rejectImplies: Design favors snappy, instantaneous updates—click triggers immediate results, no motion tweening, direct state swaps

facade.hypothesis: Health dashboards benefit from breathing room between elements to reduce cognitive load and allow users to focus on one metric at a time
facade.label: Minimal white space
facade.acceptImplies: User values calm, uncluttered interfaces; likely prefers generous padding, fewer elements per screen, zen-like layouts
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer information density, compact views, or more items visible at once; less concerned about visual rest

facade.hypothesis: A fixed left navigation paired with card-based content layout will feel organized and scannable for tracking multiple health experiments
facade.axisTargeted: Information architecture: sidebar navigation vs. tabs, and content chunking via cards vs. lists
facade.label: Sidebar plus cards
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers clear left-nav anchoring with modular, contained content blocks—likely values stability and quick visual separation between experiments
facade.rejectImplies: User may favor flat layouts, tab-based nav, or list views—prefers less structural scaffolding or denser information display
